title: The Ball in and out of Play
summary: The ball counts as out of play once it has fully crossed a goal line or touchline (on the ground or in the air), or once the referee has stopped play. It is also treated as out of play if it touches a match official and, immediately after that contact, a team gains a clear attacking opportunity, the ball ends up in the goal, or possession switches between teams — in any of these cases play restarts with a dropped ball. Outside of those situations, the ball stays in play even after touching a match official, and also remains in play if it rebounds off a goalpost, crossbar, or corner flagpost and stays on the field. If someone connected to a team but not currently playing — a coach, substitute, or a player temporarily off the pitch — touches the ball without meaning to interfere, and the ball is in the process of leaving the field anyway, the referee awards an indirect free kick rather than a more serious sanction.
source: IFAB Laws of the Game 2025/26, Law 9

title: Offside
summary: Standing in an offside position is not by itself an offence. A player is in that position if, at the moment relevant, part of their body (excluding hands and arms) is in the opponents' half and nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-to-last defender; being level with the second-to-last defender, or with the last two defenders, does not count as offside. The offence only occurs once an offside-positioned player becomes actively involved — by touching a ball played to them by a teammate, by blocking an opponent's vision or challenging for the ball, by clearly going for a nearby ball in a way that affects an opponent, or by otherwise visibly affecting an opponent's ability to play it. Gaining an advantage from a ball that comes off a post, crossbar, match official, or opponent also counts, as does benefiting from a deliberate save — but receiving the ball after an opponent deliberately played it (other than a save) does not trigger the offence. Whether a touch by the player in control of the ball was 'deliberate' is judged using factors like how far the ball travelled, its speed and predictability, and whether the player had time to control their movement. There's no offside offence on the ball coming straight from a goal kick, throw-in, or corner kick. Where it applies, the restart is an indirect free kick taken from the spot of the offence, even inside the offending team's own half.
source: IFAB Laws of the Game 2025/26, Law 11

title: Fouls and Misconduct
summary: Free kicks and penalties can only be given for offences that happen while the ball is in play. A direct free kick (or penalty, if inside the offender's own box) is awarded for physical fouls like charging, jumping at an opponent, kicking, pushing, striking, tackling carelessly/recklessly/with excessive force, tripping, holding, impeding with contact, or an illegal handball. Careless fouls draw no card; reckless ones get a yellow; excessive-force or endangering fouls get a red. Handball is judged by whether the arm moved toward the ball, whether it made the body unnaturally bigger for that situation, or whether a goal resulted directly or immediately off the hand/arm — the goalkeeper is held to the same handball standard as outfield players when outside their own box. An indirect free kick covers things like dangerous play, impeding without contact, dissent or abusive language, blocking the goalkeeper from releasing the ball, and several specific goalkeeper-handling violations inside their own area, including the 2025/26 change that a keeper holding the ball with their hands for more than eight seconds now concedes a corner kick rather than just an indirect free kick, with the referee visually counting down the final five seconds. Cautionable (yellow card) offences include unsporting behaviour such as simulation, persistent fouling, delaying restarts, and entering or leaving the field without permission; send-off (red card) offences include serious foul play, violent conduct, biting or spitting, denying an obvious goalscoring opportunity through a foul or deliberate handball, abusive language, and a second caution in the same match. Denying a clear goalscoring chance by fouling for the ball typically draws only a yellow if the offence was a genuine attempt to play the ball, but a red if it wasn't (e.g. holding or pulling).
source: IFAB Laws of the Game 2025/26, Law 12

title: The Penalty Kick (and penalty shoot-out, Law 10.3)
summary: NOTE: full Law 14 text (pp. 125-129, in-match penalty kick procedure) was not captured in this extraction pass -- the source fetch covered Law 10's penalty shoot-out procedure in detail but was truncated before reaching Law 14 itself. This summary currently only covers the penalty shoot-out (Law 10.3), not the in-match penalty kick. Re-run docling_parser.py against the full local PDF to capture true Law 14 text before treating this as complete. Shoot-out summary: a shoot-out happens after the match ends, using normal Laws unless stated otherwise; a sent-off player cannot take part, and cautions from the match don't carry over. The referee tosses a coin twice -- once for which goal is used, once for which team kicks first. Only players on the pitch (or briefly off it for treatment/equipment) at full-time are eligible, except a substitute goalkeeper if the original keeper cannot continue; teams must even up numbers if unequal at the end of regulation. During the shoot-out, only the kicker, the two goalkeepers, and match officials may be outside the centre circle. Both teams normally take five kicks alternately, each by a different player until everyone eligible has kicked once; if one team is already unbeatable before five kicks are done, the shoot-out ends early. If level after five each, it continues kick-for-kick until the scores differ after an equal number of rounds. Players can still be cautioned or sent off during the shoot-out; an ejected goalkeeper must be replaced by another eligible player, but an outfield player who can't continue simply isn't replaced.
source: IFAB Laws of the Game 2025/26, Law 10.3 (Law 14 pending re-extraction)
